label: chapter=12, username=liang12e
title: Chapter Chapter 12 Exception Handling and Text I/O

questions.text:
The following code causes Java to throw _________.

int number = Integer.MAX_VALUE + 1;
questions.explanation: In Java, arithmetic operations on primitive integer types do not throw exceptions when an overflow occurs; instead, the value wraps around according to two's complement arithmetic.

questions.text:
What exception type does the following program throw?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    System.out.println(1 / 0);
  }
}
questions.explanation: Dividing an integer by zero in Java triggers the Java Virtual Machine to throw an ArithmeticException at runtime.

questions.text: A Java exception is an instance of __________.
questions.explanation: The Throwable class is the root class for all objects that can be thrown or caught in Java, serving as the parent for both the Error and Exception classes.

questions.text: An instance of _________ describes system errors. If this type of error occurs, there is little you can do beyond notifying the user and trying to terminate the program gracefully.
questions.explanation: The Error class represents serious internal system problems, such as OutOfMemoryError, which are generally beyond the control of the application and should not typically be caught.

questions.text: An instance of _________ describes the errors caused by your program and external circumstances. These errors can be caught and handled by your program.
questions.explanation: The Exception class is intended for conditions that a reasonable application might want to catch and handle, covering both checked exceptions and runtime exceptions.

questions.text: Instances of _________ are unchecked exceptions. Please select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Unchecked exceptions consist of the Error class, the RuntimeException class, and all of their subclasses (such as NumberFormatException). These do not require explicit handling or declaration in a method's throws clause.

questions.text:
What exception type does the following program throw?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    int[] list = new int[5];
    System.out.println(list[5]);
  }
}
questions.explanation: Java arrays use zero-based indexing. For an array of size 5, the valid indices are 0 through 4; accessing index 5 is out of bounds and throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

questions.text:
What exception type does the following program throw?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    String s = "abc";
    System.out.println(s.charAt(3));
  }
}
questions.explanation: Strings in Java are zero-indexed. The string 'abc' has characters at indices 0, 1, and 2; therefore, calling charAt(3) results in a StringIndexOutOfBoundsException.

questions.text: A method must declare to throw ________.
questions.explanation: Java's 'catch or specify' requirement applies only to checked exceptions. These must either be handled within a try-catch block or declared in the method signature using the throws keyword.

questions.text:
What exception type does the following program throw?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Object o = null;
    System.out.println(o.toString());
  }
}
questions.explanation: Attempting to call an instance method, such as toString(), on a reference variable that points to null results in a NullPointerException.

questions.text:
What exception type does the following program throw?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Object o = null;
    System.out.println(o);
  }
}
questions.explanation: In Java, passing a null reference to System.out.println() does not throw an exception; instead, the PrintStream class handles the null value by converting it to the string 'null' via String.valueOf().

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: You use the keyword throws to declare exceptions in the method heading.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A method may declare multiple exceptions to be thrown.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To throw an exception, use the key word throw.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If a checked exception occurs in a method, it must be either caught or declared to be thrown from the method.

questions.explanation: All listed statements are fundamental rules of Java exception handling: 'throws' is used for declarations in method signatures, multiple exceptions can be comma-separated, 'throw' is used to trigger an exception object, and checked exceptions require the 'catch or declare' handling policy.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) throws MyException {
    System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
  }
}

class MyException extends Error {
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: You should not declare a class that extends Error, because Error raises a fatal error that terminates the program.

choices.label: B
choices.text: You cannot declare an exception in the main method.

choices.label: C
choices.text: You declared an exception in the main method, but you did not throw it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program has a compile error.

questions.explanation: While the code is syntactically correct and will compile, it is a poor design practice to extend the Error class. Error is reserved for serious system-level problems that an application should not try to handle; custom exceptions should extend the Exception class or its subclasses.

questions.text:
What exception type does the following program throw?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    Object o = new Object();
    String d = (String)o;
  }
}
questions.explanation: The program attempts to cast an instance of the base class Object to a more specific subclass String. Since the object was instantiated as an Object (new Object()) and not a String, this invalid downcasting triggers a ClassCastException at runtime.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      String s = "5.6";
      Integer.parseInt(s); // Cause a NumberFormatException

      int i = 0;
      int y = 2 / i;
    }
    catch (Exception ex) {
      System.out.println("NumberFormatException");
    }
    catch (RuntimeException ex) {
      System.out.println("RuntimeException");
    }
  }
}

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program displays NumberFormatException followed by RuntimeException.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program has a compile error.

questions.explanation: In Java, catch blocks must be ordered from the most specific exception to the most general. Because RuntimeException is a subclass of Exception, the first catch block (Exception) would catch all exceptions, making the second block (RuntimeException) unreachable, which results in a compilation error.

questions.text: An instance of _________ describes programming errors, such as bad casting, accessing an out-of-bounds array, and numeric errors.
questions.explanation: RuntimeException is the superclass of exceptions that occur during the normal operation of the Java Virtual Machine. These are often the result of programming logic errors, such as invalid array indexing (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException) or invalid casting (ClassCastException).

questions.text:
Analyze the following program.

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      String s = "5.6";
      Integer.parseInt(s); // Cause a NumberFormatException

      int i = 0;
      int y = 2 / i;
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
    }
    catch (Exception ex) {
      System.out.println(ex);
    }
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: An exception is raised due to Integer.parseInt(s);

choices.label: B
choices.text: An exception is raised due to 2 / i;

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program has a compile error.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program compiles and runs without exceptions.

questions.explanation: The execution of Integer.parseInt("5.6") immediately throws a NumberFormatException because the string contains a decimal point. This exception stops the execution of the remaining lines in the try block (including the division by zero), and control is transferred to the catch block.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      p();
      System.out.println("After the method call");
    }
    catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
      System.out.println("NumberFormatException");
    }
    catch (RuntimeException ex) {
      System.out.println("RuntimeException");
    }
  }

  static void p() {
    String s = "5.6";
    Integer.parseInt(s); // Cause a NumberFormatException

    int i = 0;
    int y = 2 / i;
    System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
  }
}

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program displays NumberFormatException followed by After the method call.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program displays NumberFormatException followed by RuntimeException.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program has a compile error.

questions.explanation: The method p() throws a NumberFormatException. This exception is caught by the first catch block in the main method that matches the exception type. Since NumberFormatException is caught specifically, 'NumberFormatException' is printed, and the code following the call to p() inside the try block is skipped.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      p();
      System.out.println("After the method call");
    }
    catch (RuntimeException ex) {
      System.out.println("RuntimeException");
    }
    catch (Exception ex) {
      System.out.println("Exception");
    }
  }

  static void p() throws Exception {
    try {
      String s = "5.6";
      Integer.parseInt(s); // Cause a NumberFormatException

      int i = 0;
      int y = 2 / i;
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
    }
    catch (RuntimeException ex) {
      System.out.println("RuntimeException");
    }
    catch (Exception ex) {
      System.out.println("Exception");
    }
  }
}

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program displays RuntimeException followed by After the method call.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program displays Exception followed by RuntimeException.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The program has a compile error.

questions.explanation: Inside method p(), the NumberFormatException is caught by the local 'catch (RuntimeException ex)' block, which prints 'RuntimeException'. Since the exception is handled locally, p() completes normally, allowing the main method to continue and print 'After the method call'.

questions.text:
What is wrong in the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main (String[] args) {
    try {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
     }
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: You cannot have a try block without a catch block.

choices.label: B
choices.text: You cannot have a try block without a catch block or a finally block.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A method call that does not declare exceptions cannot be placed inside a try block.

questions.explanation: The Java language specification requires every 'try' block to be followed by at least one 'catch' block or a 'finally' block. A 'try' block alone is syntactically incomplete and will cause a compilation error.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main (String[] args) {
    try {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
    }
    finally {
      System.out.println("The finally clause is executed");
    }
  }
}

choices.label: B
choices.text: Welcome to Java followed by The finally clause is executed in the next line

questions.explanation: The finally block is designed to execute regardless of whether an exception is thrown or not in the try block. In this code, the try block completes successfully, printing 'Welcome to Java', and then the finally block executes, printing 'The finally clause is executed'.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main (String[] args) {
    try {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
      return;
    }
    finally {
      System.out.println("The finally clause is executed");
    }
  }
}

choices.label: B
choices.text: Welcome to Java followed by The finally clause is executed in the next line

questions.explanation: The finally block executes even if the try block contains a return statement. The control flow ensures that the finally code runs before the method actually returns to the caller, resulting in both lines being printed.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
      int i = 0;
      int y = 2 / i;
      System.out.println("Welcome to HTML");
    }
    finally {
      System.out.println("The finally clause is executed");
    }
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: Welcome to Java, then an error message.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Welcome to Java followed by The finally clause is executed in the next line, then an error message.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program displays three lines: Welcome to Java, Welcome to HTML, The finally clause is executed, then an error message.

questions.explanation: When the integer division by zero occurs, an ArithmeticException is thrown. The try block stops immediately (skipping 'Welcome to HTML'), the finally block executes ('The finally clause is executed'), and since the exception is not caught, the JVM then prints the error message.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
      int i = 0;
      double y = 2.0 / i;
      System.out.println("Welcome to HTML");
    }
    finally {
      System.out.println("The finally clause is executed");
    }
  }
}

choices.label: B
choices.text: Welcome to Java followed by The finally clause is executed in the next line.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program displays three lines: Welcome to Java, Welcome to HTML, The finally clause is executed.

questions.explanation: In Java, floating-point division by zero (2.0 / 0) does not throw an exception; instead, it results in a value of Infinity. Consequently, the try block completes normally, printing both 'Welcome' lines, followed by the finally block.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
      int i = 0;
      int y = 2 / i;
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
    }
    catch (RuntimeException ex) {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
    }
    finally {
      System.out.println("End of the block");
    }
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java three times followed by End of the block.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java two times followed by End of the block.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java three times.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java two times.

questions.explanation: The first line is printed, then an ArithmeticException (a subclass of RuntimeException) is thrown. The catch block intercepts it and prints the second line, and finally, the finally block executes to print 'End of the block'.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
      int i = 0;
      int y = 2 / i;
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
    }
    catch (RuntimeException ex) {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
    }
    finally {
      System.out.println("End of the block");
    }
   
    System.out.println("End of the block");
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java three times followed by End of the block.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java two times followed by End of the block.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java two times followed by End of the block two times.

questions.explanation: This program follows the same logic as the previous one, but because the exception was caught and handled, the program execution continues normally after the try-catch-finally structure, executing the final print statement.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
      int i = 0;
      int y = 2 / i;
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
    }
    finally {
      System.out.println("End of the block");
    }
   
    System.out.println("End of the block");
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java three times followed by End of the block.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java two times followed by End of the block.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java two times followed by End of the block two times.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program displays Welcome to Java and End of the block, and then terminates because of an unhandled exception.

questions.explanation: Because there is no catch block, the ArithmeticException stops the try block and jumps to the finally block. After 'End of the block' is printed, the exception remains unhandled, so the program terminates immediately, never reaching the final print statement outside the block.

questions.text: Which of the following is not an advantage of Java exception handling?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Java separates exception handling from normal processing tasks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exception handling makes it possible for the caller's caller to handle the exception.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exception handling simplifies programming because the error-reporting and error-handling code can be placed at the catch block.

questions.explanation: Exception handling is used to create robust, maintainable code by separating error-handling logic from business logic. However, it typically decreases performance due to the overhead of creating exception objects and unwinding the call stack.

questions.text:
Analyze the following code:

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      int zero = 0;
      int y = 2 / zero;
      try {
        String s = "5.6";
        Integer.parseInt(s); // Cause a NumberFormatException
      }
      catch(Exception e) {
      }
    }
    catch(RuntimeException e) {
      System.out.println(e);
    }
  }
}

choices.label: A
choices.text: A try-catch block cannot be embedded inside another try-catch block.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A good programming practice is to avoid nesting try-catch blocks, because nesting makes programs difficult to read. You can rewrite the program using only one try-catch block.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program has a compile error because Exception appears before RuntimeException.

questions.explanation: While nesting try-catch blocks is syntactically legal in Java, it is generally discouraged as a programming practice because it makes the logic harder to follow and maintain compared to a flattened structure.

questions.text:
What is displayed on the console when running the following program?

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    try {
      method();
      System.out.println("After the method call");
    }
    catch (RuntimeException ex) {
      System.out.println("RuntimeException");
    }
    catch (Exception ex) {
      System.out.println("Exception");
    }
  }

  static void method() throws Exception {
    try {
      String s = "5.6";
      Integer.parseInt(s); // Cause a NumberFormatException

      int i = 0;
      int y = 2 / i;
      System.out.println("Welcome to Java");
    }
    catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
      System.out.println("NumberFormatException");
      throw ex;
    }
    catch (RuntimeException ex) {
      System.out.println("RuntimeException");
    }
  }
}

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program displays NumberFormatException followed by After the method call.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The program displays NumberFormatException followed by RuntimeException.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The program has a compile error.

questions.explanation: In method(), a NumberFormatException is caught, printed, and then re-thrown. In main(), this re-thrown exception is caught by the RuntimeException catch block (since NumberFormatException inherits from RuntimeException) and 'RuntimeException' is printed.

questions.text: What are the reasons to create an instance of the File class? Please select all that apply.

choices.label: A
choices.text: To determine whether the file exists.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To obtain the properties of the file such as whether the file can be read, written, or is hidden.

choices.label: E
choices.text: To read/write data from/to a file

questions.explanation: The File class is used for managing file system metadata and performing file-level operations like checking existence, retrieving properties, renaming, and deleting; however, it does not handle the actual reading or writing of file content.

questions.text: Which of the following is used to test if a file c:\temp\test.txt exists?
questions.explanation: In Java string literals, the backslash is an escape character, so a literal backslash must be represented as '\\'. Additionally, the correct method for checking existence is 'exists()', not 'exist()'.

questions.text: Which of the following statements creates an instance of File in Windows for the file c:\temp.txt?
questions.explanation: To represent a Windows file path in a Java string literal, backslashes must be escaped with another backslash (e.g., 'c:\\temp.txt'). While forward slashes also work in many Java environments, the question specifically targets the escaped backslash syntax.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If a file (e.g., c:\temp.txt) does not exist, new File("c:\\temp.txt") returns null.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If a directory (e.g., c:\liang) does not exist, new File("c:\liang") returns null.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If a file (e.g., c:\temp.txt) does not exist, new File("c:\\temp.txt") creates a new file named c:\temp.txt.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If a directory (e.g., c:\liang) does not exist, new File("c:\liang") creates a new directory named c:\liang.

questions.explanation: The File constructor does not interact with the physical disk; it simply creates an object in memory representing a path. It does not return null or create files/directories automatically regardless of whether the path exists.

questions.text: Which class do you use to write data into a text file?
questions.explanation: The PrintWriter class is the standard class used for writing formatted text data to a file in Java.

questions.text: Which method can be used to write data?
questions.explanation: The 'print' and 'println' methods are the standard methods provided by classes like PrintWriter to write data to a destination.

questions.text: Which method can be used to create an output object for file temp.txt? Please select all that apply.
questions.explanation: The PrintWriter class provides overloaded constructors that allow you to create an output object using either a String representing the file path (A) or a File object (C).

questions.text: Which class contains the method for checking whether a file exists?
questions.explanation: The 'exists()' method, which checks for the presence of a file or directory on the disk, is a member of the java.io.File class.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements are correct? Please select all that apply.

I: 
try (PrintWriter output = new PrintWriter("output.txt");) {
  output.println("Welcome to Java");
}

II: 
try (PrintWriter output = new PrintWriter("output.txt");
     Scanner input = new Scanner(System.in);) {
  output.println(input.nextLine());
}

III: 
PrintWriter output;
try (output = new PrintWriter("output.txt");) {
  output.println("Welcome to Java");
}

IV: 
try (PrintWriter output = new PrintWriter("output.txt");) {
  output.println("Welcome to Java");
}
finally {
  output.close();
}
questions.explanation: Statements I and II are correct because try-with-resources requires the resource to be declared within the parentheses. Statement III is incorrect because the resource is declared outside, and Statement IV is incorrect because the resource variable is not in scope within the finally block.

questions.text: Which class do you use to read data from a text file?
questions.explanation: The Scanner class is used to read and parse text data from various sources, including files, by passing a File object to its constructor.

questions.text: Which method can be used to read a whole line from the file?
questions.explanation: The nextLine() method of the Scanner class reads all characters until a line separator is encountered and returns them as a string, effectively reading a whole line.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements are correct?

I: 
File file = new File("input.txt");
try (Scanner input = new Scanner(file);) {
  String line = input.nextLine();
}

II: 
try (File file = new File("input.txt");
     Scanner input = new Scanner(file);) {
  String line = input.nextLine();
}

III: 
File file;
try (file = new File("input.txt");
     Scanner input = new Scanner(file);) {
  String line = input.nextLine();
}

IV: 
File file;
Scanner input;
try (file = new File("input.txt");
     input = new Scanner(file);) {
  String line = input.nextLine();
}
questions.explanation: The try-with-resources statement requires the resource to implement the AutoCloseable interface. Scanner implements this, but File does not. Additionally, resources must be initialized within the parentheses to be managed by the try-with-resources block.

questions.text: Which method can be used to create an input object for file temp.txt?
questions.explanation: To read from a file, the Scanner constructor must be passed a java.io.File object. Passing a string literal directly, as in 'new Scanner("temp.txt")', creates a scanner that reads the string content itself rather than the contents of the file named temp.txt.

questions.text:
Suppose you enter 34.3 57.8 789, then press the ENTER key. Analyze the following code.

Scanner input = new Scanner(System.in);
int v1 = input.nextInt();
int v2 = input.nextInt();
String line = input.nextLine();

choices.label: A
choices.text: After the last statement is executed, v1 is 34.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The program has a runtime error because 34.3 is not an integer.

choices.label: C
choices.text: After the last statement is executed, line contains characters '7', '8', '9', '\n'.

choices.label: D
choices.text: After the last statement is executed, line contains characters '7', '8', '9'.

questions.explanation: The nextInt() method expects the next token to be a valid integer. Since '34.3' contains a decimal point, it is treated as a double, and calling nextInt() on it results in an InputMismatchException at runtime.

questions.text:
Suppose you enter 34.3 57.8 789, then press the ENTER key. Analyze the following code.

Scanner input = new Scanner(System.in);
double v1 = input.nextDouble();
double v2 = input.nextDouble();
String line = input.nextLine();

choices.label: A
choices.text: After the last statement is executed, line contains characters '7', '8', '9'.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After the last statement is executed, line contains characters '7', '8', '9', '\n'.

choices.label: C
choices.text: After the last statement is executed, line contains characters ' ', '7', '8', '9', '\n'.

choices.label: D
choices.text: After the last statement is executed, line contains characters ' ', '7', '8', '9'.

questions.explanation: After two nextDouble() calls read '34.3' and '57.8', the scanner cursor is positioned exactly after the '8'. The remaining characters on the line are the space followed by '7', '8', and '9'. The nextLine() method reads from the current cursor position to the end of the line.

questions.text:
Suppose you enter 34.3, the ENTER key, 57.8, the ENTER key. Analyze the following code. Please select all that apply.

Line 1  Scanner input = new Scanner(System.in);
Line 2  double v1 = input.nextDouble();  
Line 3  double v2 = input.nextDouble();
Line 4  String line = input.nextLine();

choices.label: A
choices.text: After line 2 is executed, v1 is 34.3.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After line 3 is executed, v2 is 57.8.

choices.label: C
choices.text: After line 4 is executed, line contains an empty string.

choices.label: D
choices.text: After line 4 is executed, line is null.

choices.label: E
choices.text: After line 4 is executed, line contains character "\n".

questions.explanation: The nextDouble() method consumes the numeric token but stops before the line separator (ENTER). After '57.8' is read, the cursor is at the newline character. The nextLine() method then consumes this newline character and returns the empty string preceding it.

questions.text:
Suppose you enter 34.3, the ENTER key, 57.8, the ENTER key, abc, the Enter key. Analyze the following code. Please select all that apply.

Line 1  Scanner input = new Scanner(System.in);
Line 2  double v1 = input.nextDouble();  
Line 3  double v2 = input.nextDouble();
Line 4  String line = input.nextLine();

choices.label: A
choices.text: After line 2 is executed, v1 is 34.3.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After line 3 is executed, v2 is 57.8.

choices.label: C
choices.text: After line 4 is executed, line contains an empty string.

choices.label: D
choices.text: After line 4 is executed, line is null.

choices.label: E
choices.text: After line 4 is executed, line contains character "abc".

questions.explanation: After nextDouble() reads '57.8', the scanner's cursor sits at the end of that numeric token, before the first ENTER key character. Consequently, nextLine() consumes the remaining empty space and the newline character on that specific line, leaving 'abc' in the buffer for subsequent read operations.

questions.text: To create an InputStream to read from a file on a Web server, you use the method __________ in the URL class.
questions.explanation: The URL class provides the openStream() method, which opens a connection to the URL and returns an InputStream for reading its contents.
